name: Geographies of Despair: Gender & Governance
description: Join us for a lecture that asks what citizenship means when you live in a place where belonging itself is contested.About the LectureBorders are often imagined as lines on maps. In reality, they are lived spaces—shaped by militarisation, surveillance, uncertainty, and competing claims of nationhood.This lecture examines the gendered realities of life in India's conflict regions and borderlands. It begins with a simple but often overlooked observation: war and conflict are never gender-neutral. They produce different vulnerabilities, forms of violence, and experiences of everyday life for different people.Drawing on decades of research in regions marked by conflict and political contestation, the session explores:How conflict and militarisation reshape everyday lifeWhy women often experience violence, security, and citizenship differentlyWhat belonging means in regions where sovereignty is disputedHow governance operates in spaces marked by exceptional laws and extraordinary state powerAt its heart, this lecture asks what happens when modern ideas of citizenship, security, and nationhood collide with the realities of people living at the margins of the state.About the SpeakerSeema Kazi is Associate Professor at the Centre for Women's Development Studies (CWDS), New Delhi. Educated in India, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom, she holds an MA in Women in Development Studies from the Institute of Social Studies and a PhD from the Gender Institute at the London School of Economics.Her work spans gender, conflict studies, governance, minority rights, and international relations. She is the author of In Kashmir: Gender, Militarization and the Modern Nation-State and Gender and Governance: Perspectives from South Asia. For over two decades, her research has examined how power, citizenship, and belonging are experienced in regions marked by conflict and political uncertainty.Your ticket includes a 250/- coupon redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: SideRoom: Nature Morte x unLecture
description: For the first session of Side Room, a collaborative programme by Nature Morte and unLecture, artist Remen Chopra W. Van Der Vaart reflects on a practice shaped by material, memory, landscape, and time.Drawing from works currently on view in Shapes of Time alongside earlier bodies of work, the conversation moves through an artistic language built from materials that arrive carrying their own histories: recycled wood, lapis lazuli, carpet, and surfaces marked by erosion, accumulation, and labour.Centred around ideas of mapping, belonging, and emotional geography, the session explores how memory becomes embedded within material and how landscapes hold traces of both personal and collective histories. From early years rooted in drawing and observation to later spatial and architectural influences, the conversation offers an intimate look into the references, processes, and lived experiences that continue to shape the artist's work.Rather than a formal lecture, Side Room invites audiences into the quieter spaces around artistic practice — the questions, materials, memories, and moments that exist behind finished works.About the ArtistRemen Chopra W. Van Der Vaart (b. 1980, New Delhi) is an interdisciplinary artist whose practice explores migration, memory, identity, and the passage of time.Working across photography, drawing, sculpture, textiles, and sound, she develops a poetic visual language grounded in questions of home, cultural landscapes, and intergenerational memory. Her work navigates the space between the physical and the emotional, reflecting on displacement, belonging, fragility, regeneration, and cyclical notions of time.Her work has been exhibited internationally at institutions and major platforms including Musée Guimet, Paris; The Gujral Foundation, New Delhi; Nature Morte, New Delhi; 1x1 Art Gallery, Dubai; the Venice Biennale (Transnational Pavilion); Kochi–Muziris Biennale; Dhaka Art Summit; and Asia Now, Paris.

name: Drinks that Changed the World
description: Dawa DaaruHow Alcohol Helped Shape Empires, Religions, Revolutions, and Everyday Life Join us for a lecture that begins with a drink and ends with a completely different way of looking at history.About the LectureWhat if some of the most important events in world history were shaped not only by kings, wars, and ideas — but also by what people were drinking?From ancient India and Mesopotamia to Mughal courts, colonial trade routes, and modern nation-states, alcohol has quietly influenced politics, religion, commerce, culture, and empire in ways most history books rarely discuss.This lecture traces a wildly unexpected journey through thousands of years of human civilisation, exploring how beverages became entangled with power, identity, conquest, and resistance.The session explores:Ancient references to intoxicating drinks in Indian epics and mythologyThe world's earliest known drinking cultures and the famous Hymn to NinkasiWhy Champagne became synonymous with luxury and prestigeHow coffee, tea, wine, gin, beer, and spirits reshaped empires and trade routesThe surprising role alcohol played in colonial expansion and global commerceDrinking cultures of the Mughals, poets, soldiers, revolutionaries, and rulersMoving between historical anecdotes, forgotten stories, literary references, travel experiences, and cultural curiosities, the lecture reveals alcohol not simply as a beverage, but as a lens through which to understand human history itself.About the SpeakerGagan Sharma is a certified sommelier, mixologist, wine educator, writer, and beverage storyteller with over a decade of experience across India and abroad.His work sits at the intersection of beverage culture, travel, history, and education. Over the years, he has worked as a wine educator, tasting curator, industry trainer, judge, and writer, helping audiences understand the stories, traditions, and craftsmanship behind what they drink.Known as India's first sommelier, Gagan also holds a record for his travels across UNESCO World Heritage Sites in India. Beyond the world of beverages, he is a certified mountaineer, paragliding pilot, passionate student of Mughal history, and enthusiastic explorer of heritage landscapes.His lectures combine deep historical research with personal stories, making complex histories feel lively, accessible, and unexpectedly entertaining.Your ticket includes a complimentary cocktail inspired by the themes of the lecture.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: FishMarket: Movement, Health & the City We Create
description: FishMarket #1 - Why Is Getting Around Delhi So Hard?A new public conversation series by India Development Foundation × unLectureAbout the EventMost public discussions happen in one of two ways.Experts speak. Everyone else listens.Or everyone complains. Nobody learns anything.The FishMarket is an attempt at something different. Created by the India Development Foundation (IDF) and unLecture, it brings researchers, policymakers, practitioners, and citizens into the same room to think through public problems together.Each session begins with a short introduction from experts working on a particular issue. Then the floor opens up.Questions. Frustrations. Disagreements. Lived experiences. The audience drives the conversation.Think of it as a town hall, a public salon, and a café conversation rolled into one. For our first Fish Market, we're asking a question most Delhiites encounter every single day: Why is getting around the city SO difficult?Traffic congestion, unsafe streets, public transport, pedestrian infrastructure, cycling, pollution, noise, road design, commuting, accessibility, mobility policy - nothing is off limits.The goal is to leave with a deeper understanding of how these systems work, why certain problems persist, and what meaningful solutions might actually look like.The SpeakersGeetam Tiwari: She is an Emeritus Professor at the Transportation Research and Injury Prevention (TRIP) Centre at IIT Delhi and one of India's foremost experts on transport planning and road safety.For over three decades, her work has focused on the movement of people through cities, particularly pedestrians, cyclists, public transport systems, and vulnerable road users. Her research has shaped national and international conversations on sustainable urban mobility and traffic safety.She is the recipient of numerous national and international honours, including the 2024 International Distinguished Career Award from the American Public Health Association.Girish Agrawal: He is a Professor at the TRIP Centre, IIT Delhi, where his work sits at the intersection of engineering, law, policy, and transportation safety.His research focuses on vulnerable road users, transport regulation, and the hidden consequences of mobility systems. Most recently, he has been studying the impact of road traffic noise on vendors, pedestrians, and informal transport workers.Before returning to India, Girish spent over two decades working on transport infrastructure projects in California. He holds degrees in engineering, law, and public policy, and brings a uniquely interdisciplinary perspective to questions of urban mobility.Moderated By: Shubhashis Gangopadhyay Economist, public intellectual, and Chairman of the India Development Foundation.About IDFThe India Development Foundation (IDF) is a research organisation working on questions central to India's development story: the economy, technology, urbanisation, innovation, governance, markets, human capital, and public policy.Its work is rooted in the belief that rigorous research can contribute to better public conversations and better policy outcomes.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars, and neighbourhood spaces across the city.Created by three friends from St. Stephen's College, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social, and alive, unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Delhi Before the Fall - History of 19th century
description: Kathika is delighted to host an engaging Baithak with Shah Umair, the creator of the widely followed platform Sikkawala, known for bringing the history of Delhi to life through compelling storytelling, archival research and rare visual material.Titled “Delhi Before the Fall – The History of 19th Century Delhi”, this Baithak invites participants to journey into a city standing at the threshold of profound transformation. The nineteenth century was one of the most dramatic periods in Delhi’s history — a time when the city still retained the cultural brilliance of the Mughal world, even as political power shifted and colonial influence steadily expanded.Through the intimate setting of a Baithak, Shah Umair will explore the social, cultural and urban life of Delhi before the upheavals that reshaped the city forever. Drawing upon forgotten narratives, the session will paint a vivid picture of a city that was at once refined, resilient and deeply interconnected.From the bustling bazaars of Shahjahanabad and the lives of its residents to the literary circles, architectural landmarks and changing political realities of the era, the conversation will uncover layers of a Delhi that existed before the rupture of 1857 and the transformations that followed. The session seeks to move beyond dates and events to understand the rhythms of everyday life, the people who inhabited the city and the cultural world they created.

name: Reminiscing Sa'di: A Legacy of Persian Literature
description: Join us for a lecture that traces one of the most influential cultural exchanges in South Asian history — a centuries-long relationship between India and Persia that shaped the languages we speak, the literature we read, and the ways power itself was imagined.About the LectureFor centuries, Persian was not a foreign language in India.It was the language of courts and administration, poetry and philosophy, diplomacy and social mobility. Long before English occupied that role, Persian connected vast parts of the subcontinent to a broader Persianate world stretching from Anatolia to Central Asia.Its influence was so deep that generations of Indian scholars, poets, bureaucrats, and intellectuals began their education through Persian. Communities such as Brahmins, Kayasthas, and Khatris built careers through their mastery of the language, while poets from Iran travelled to India seeking patronage, literary fame, and intellectual companionship.This lecture explores the remarkable journey of Persian in India — not simply as an imported language, but as a tradition transformed by the people who adopted it.The session explores:Why Persian became the language of power and culture in HindustanThe lives and journeys of Persian poets who made India their homeHow Indian scholars and writers reshaped Persian literary traditionsThe ethical and political worlds contained in texts by Sa'di, Hafiz, Rumi, and othersThe influence of Persian on Hindi, Urdu, and other Indian vernacularsHow Persian civilisation retained its distinct identity across centuries of political and religious changeMoving between history, literature, politics, and storytelling, the lecture reveals a world in which ideas, languages, and people moved across borders far more freely than we often imagine today.At its heart, this is a story about exchange: how cultures borrow, adapt, and reinvent one another — and how those encounters continue to echo in our everyday lives.About the SpeakersDr. Mukhtar Ahmed teaches at the Department of Persian, University of Delhi, where his research focuses on Modern Persian Literature and Indo-Persian literary traditions. His forthcoming book, From Persia to Paris: A Journey of Ideas and Cultures, examines the intellectual and literary exchanges between Persian and French traditions. Known among friends for both his scholarship and his cooking, he remains deeply interested in the movement of ideas across languages and civilisations.Heena Goswami is a public policy professional with an academic background in history and has recently submitted her PhD dissertation at the University of Delhi. Born and raised in Rajasthan, her interests span history, politics, technology, and public life. An avid reader and former educator, she now spends much of her time encouraging others to read, often through stories about books and ideas shared online.Your ticket includes a 300/- coupon redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: Daughters of Shahjahan at Red fort (A Heritage walk)
description: Daughters of Shahjahan Heritage walk at the Redfort (With Dramatic Reading of play excerpts)Join Darwesh Heritage Walks & Sabhyata Foundation for a unique Immersive experience at the Red Fort .The Red Fort (Lal Qila), an iconic symbol of India's history, stands proudly in Old Delhi as a silent witness to centuries of political, architectural, and cultural change. Commissioned by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in 1638, this UNESCO World Heritage Site is not just a monument—it is a storyteller, narrating tales of imperial grandeur, colonial resistance, and post-independence pride. Stories about the Mughal opulence, the power politics, the powerful place of women in our history will unfold as we walk through the bygone Mughal era.The ‘war of succession’ that took place among the four sons of Shah Jahan had one novelty, while previously, wars were fought after the death of the emperor, this war was fought when Shah Jahan was still alive. All sons and daughters of Shah Jahan participated in it. While the four sons Dara Shikoh, Shah Shuja, Aurangzeb and Murad Baksh fought against each other, the sisters Jahanara and Roshanara allied themselves with one or the other brother. Through this we will bring to you to the story of the Mughal battle of succession that changed the course of Indian history.The heritage walk includes (dramatic reading of play excerpts in Hindustani) highlighting stories and scenes from the tussle going on between Shahjahan’s Sons Aurangzeb and Dara Shikoh and the two daughters Jahanara and Roshanara Begum, each of whom sided with one brother.

name: Kuch Kuch Queer Hai
description: Join us for a lecture that begins with a question Bollywood has spent decades avoiding: Where are all the queer rom-coms?About the LectureBollywood has given us countless love stories—epic romances, disastrous love triangles, dramatic reunions, and unforgettable meet-cutes. Yet queer love has rarely been allowed to occupy the centre of the frame.This lecture traces the curious relationship between Bollywood, queerness, and the romantic comedy. While queer movements, legal struggles, and literature have transformed how queer lives are imagined in India, cinema has often struggled to keep pace.Drawing on queer theory, genre studies, and film history, the session explores:Why the rom-com has historically excluded queer love storiesHow censorship controversies shaped representations of sexuality on screenWhat streaming platforms have changed—and what they haven'tThe political and emotional possibilities of a genuinely queer Bollywood romanceMoving between films, legal history, and cultural criticism, the lecture asks a deceptively simple question: What would happen if Bollywood finally allowed queer love to get its happily-ever-after?About the SpeakerDr. Kashish Dua is Assistant Professor of English at Jesus and Mary College, University of Delhi. A Fulbright-Nehru Doctoral Fellow, she was a visiting researcher at the University of California, Berkeley from 2023–24.Her research explores the intersections of queer literary studies, citizenship studies, and legal theory. Her work has been published by Oxford University Press, Routledge, Peter Lang, and DK London. She is also the co-editor of Vernacular Encounters: Politics and Possibilities (Routledge, 2026).An academic by day and a devoted rom-com enthusiast by night, she insists the two pursuits have far more in common than most people think.Your ticket includes a 250/- coupon redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: The Making Of A Serial Offender
description: Join us for an evening that begins with a simple question: what makes a serial offender? The answer may be more unsettling than you think.About the LectureThis lecture explores how criminal profiling moved beyond outdated assumptions about "criminal faces" to become one of the most fascinating tools in modern investigations. Drawing from forensic psychology, criminology, and real-world case studies, the session examines how investigators attempt to understand offenders through behaviour rather than appearance.Together, we'll explore:Why humans are often poor judges of who is capable of violenceThe evolution of criminal profiling, from early theories to modern investigative psychologyHow crime scene behaviour can reveal patterns of motive, planning, control, and escalationThe origins of behavioural profiling and the rise of the FBI's Behavioural Science UnitWhat famous serial offender cases reveal about fantasy, victim selection, and criminal developmentWhy serial offending remains understudied in IndiaHow forensic psychologists and investigators use Behavioural Evidence Analysis to reconstruct offender behaviourMoving beyond sensationalism and popular crime shows, this lecture examines the deeper psychological and social questions that profiling raises.Why do some individuals cross lines that most never do? What can behaviour tell us about risk, intent, and future offending? And how can India build more rigorous behavioural science frameworks for criminal investigation?Part psychology, part criminology, and part investigative science, this session offers a rare glimpse into the complex process of understanding criminal behaviour.About the SpeakerMr. Rithin Joseph is an Assistant Professor at the School of Behavioural Forensics, National Forensic Sciences University (NFSU), Delhi.Specialising in Criminology and Forensic Psychology, his work focuses on criminal profiling, deception detection, investigative psychology, and forensic behavioural analysis. He has published multiple research papers and presented over twenty-one papers at national and international conferences.Mr. Joseph has specialised training in a range of forensic investigative techniques, including Polygraph, Brain Electrical Oscillation Signature Profiling (BEOS), Suspect Detection System (SDS), Layered Voice Analysis (LVA), and EyeDetect.He currently serves as Co-Investigator on an ICSSR-funded research project examining the prediction of recidivism among first-time offenders in conflict with the law. He is also the co-author of *Crime as the Cause of Criminality* and *BRICS: Unravelling the Abyss*.A Gold Medalist in Criminology from the National Forensic Sciences University and recipient of the **Young Criminologist Award 2024**, he continues to contribute to research in criminal psychology, offender behaviour, and investigative science while mentoring postgraduate students and pursuing doctoral research in Criminal Psychology.Your ticket includes an alcoholic/non-alcoholic beverage of your choice!About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: Sex, Death, And The Long War Of Life
description: Join us for a lecture that begins with a provocative proposition:You were never meant to exist.And that may be the most liberating fact about you.About the LectureHuman beings like stories.We tell ourselves that life has meaning, that history bends toward progress, that our desires are uniquely our own.Evolution tells a different story.One without heroes, villains, purpose, or justice.This lecture explores the oldest conflict on Earth: the endless struggle of living things to survive, reproduce, and persist. Long before nations, religions, or political ideologies existed, life was already engaged in a ruthless competition that continues to shape every organism alive today.Drawing on evolutionary biology and psychology, the session traces how some of our deepest instincts emerged from this ancient inheritance.The lecture explores:Why evolution remains the most powerful framework for understanding lifeHow competition and cooperation coexist in the natural worldWhat sex is really doing from the perspective of evolutionWhy human beings are, like all organisms, unfinished creaturesHow morality, consciousness, and even the idea of a “soul” might be viewed through an evolutionary lensMoving from algae to human behaviour, from reproduction to mortality, the session challenges many of the comforting assumptions we hold about ourselves.At its heart lies a difficult but strangely freeing question:What changes when we stop imagining that life was designed for us?About the SpeakerAmbarish Satwik is a Delhi-based vascular and endovascular surgeon, Director of the Vascular Cath Lab at Sir Ganga Ram Hospital, and Professor of Vascular Surgery at GRIPMER.Alongside his medical career, he is an acclaimed writer whose work moves across literature, history, politics, and science. His novel Perineum: Nether Parts of the Empire is a critically acclaimed exploration of sexuality and power in the British Raj.His essays, columns, and commentaries have appeared in publications including India Today, The Times of India, Outlook, Open, Tehelka, Time Out, and Hindu Business Line.Known for bringing together scientific rigour, intellectual irreverence, and dark humour, his talks often leave audiences questioning ideas they previously took for granted.Ticket IncludesYour ticket includes a complimentary drink during the session.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Through thoughtfully moderated sessions, we'll reflect on the role of cafés as contemporary cultural anchors and uncover the personal stories behind the city's most cherished discoveries.Coffee Anchor examines how cafés become more than places to drink coffee—evolving into landmarks of creativity, community, and everyday belonging.Social Move invites guests to share memorable city encounters, summer rituals, and the spaces that have left a lasting imprint on their lives.Set against a summer backdrop, the evening offers a slower pace, meaningful dialogue, and the opportunity to connect with others who appreciate the nuances of urban culture.Summer Coffee Crawl 2025Stop 1 — Summer in the City14 June | Edit5, Sector 64, GurugramStop 2 — Coffee & Confessions21 June | Alayna Café, Shahpur JatStop 3 — Mode of Transport28 June | ATE Glasshouse, Lodhi RoadTickets- Single stop pass: Access to the event at that particular stop of the crawl - Three stop pass: Access to all 3 stops of the crawl

name: The Procrastireaders
description: The Procrastireaders: Love is Love - Pride Month ReadingOverviewA slow reading circle designed around comfort, not pressure.People come together to read, exchange thoughts, and return to books in a way that feels lighter and more approachable. Stories, shared slowly.This June, Procrastireaders is reading stories about queer love. The joy, the tenderness, the ordinary and extraordinary ways people find each other. Stories where being queer is not the conflict to be resolved but simply part of who the characters are. If that sounds like your kind of read, come join us.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to ExpectDiscussion First: We open with a conversation around the theme, the books you brought, and what drew you to them. No right answers, just an honest exchange.Silent Reading Block: We close with dedicated quiet time to actually sit with your book. Good lighting, AC, and fellow readers for company.Bring Your Own Book: Carry whatever you are currently reading or something from your "to be read" pile that needs your attention.Details🗓️ Date: 27th June (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Unlocked, 32nd Avenue🎟️ Fee: ₹700

name: 4D Polymers: 3 Dimensions & Beyond
description: Join us for a lecture that explores a future where materials don't simply exist, they respond, transform, heal, and even generate their own energy.About the LectureMost materials are passive.A bandage covers a wound. An implant supports damaged tissue. A scaffold holds cells in place.But what if materials could do more?What if they could change shape over time, respond to their environment, generate electrical signals, and actively participate in the healing process?This lecture introduces the emerging world of 4D biomaterials — materials designed not only in three dimensions, but with a fourth dimension: time.Drawing from cutting-edge research in regenerative medicine and bioengineering, the session explores how scientists are creating materials that can mimic some of the remarkable properties of living tissue. These next-generation biomaterials are capable of programmed transformations, self-powered stimulation, and dynamic interactions with the cells around them.The lecture explores:What 4D printing is and how it differs from conventional 3D printingHow materials can be designed to change shape and function over timeThe role of bioelectric signals in tissue healing and regenerationHow self-powered biomaterials can generate electrical stimulation without batteries or external energy sourcesWhy these technologies could transform the future of healthcare, implants, and regenerative medicineAlong the way, the session offers a glimpse into a future where materials are no longer passive tools but active participants in biological processes.About the SpeakerDr. Amit Nain is an Assistant Professor at the Kusuma School of Biological Sciences, IIT Delhi, where he leads research focused on developing next-generation biomaterials for tissue regeneration and advanced healthcare applications.His laboratory works at the intersection of materials science, biology, and engineering, designing bioinstructive materials that can sense, respond, and support healing processes within the body.Dr. Nain completed his B.Tech. in Biotechnology from VIT University and earned his PhD in Nano Science and Technology from National Taiwan University, where his research focused on antimicrobial nanomaterials.His work has been recognised internationally, including being named a 2024 Emerging Investigator by the Royal Society of Chemistry and a 2025 Rising Star by the American Chemical Society. He has published over 35 research articles, book chapters, and conference proceedings, and currently serves on the Early Career Board of Biomacromolecules.Outside the lab, he is a Bollywood enthusiast, poetry lover, road-trip addict, and an unapologetic fan of Friends, Breaking Bad, Homeland, and Money Heist.Your ticket includes a 200/- voucher for F&B About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: The Hidden History Of Queer Lives
description: Join us for a lecture that challenges one of the most persistent myths about queerness in India: that it is new.About the LectureHistory, as it is usually told, rarely makes room for queer lives.Queer people appear as footnotes, court cases, scandals, anthropological curiosities, or cautionary tales. Entire worlds of belonging, intimacy, resistance, and community are left out of the official record.But they were always here.This lecture traces a longer and richer history of queerness in India—one that stretches far beyond contemporary debates about identity, rights, and representation.Moving through colonial archives, legal history, community formations, and political movements, the session explores:What existed before Section 377—and what colonial rule sought to eraseHow communities such as hijras, kothis, aravanis, and jogatis built their own social worldsThe long struggle for queer rights in IndiaWhy remembering queer histories changes how we understand the presentAnchored by the anthology Queer India Now, this lecture is ultimately about memory itself: who gets remembered, who gets forgotten, and what becomes possible when lost histories are recovered.About the SpeakersDhamini Ratnam is National Culture Editor at Hindustan Times and an award-winning journalist specialising in gender and sexuality. Over a seventeen-year career spanning publications such as Mint, Mumbai Mirror, and Mid-Day, she has written extensively about LGBTQIA+ lives in India and broader questions of social change.Dhrubo Jyoti is a Delhi-based writer and editor whose work focuses on questions of caste, queerness, and representation. Their work seeks to foreground voices and histories that have often remained at the margins of mainstream LGBTQIA+ narratives.Your ticket includes a 250/- coupon redemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: Night Heritage walk :  Humayun's Tomb & Museum
description: Step into the grandeur of Humayun's Tomb with us for an immersive Night heritage walk that goes beyond stone and symmetry.Curated by Darwesh Heritage Walks in collaboration with Sabhyata Foundation.We will not only explore the monument’s magnificent architecture, its Persian influences, and its place in the evolution of Mughal design — but also rediscover Humayun himself, perhaps the most underestimated of the Mughals. His struggles, exile, return, and legacy shaped the empire in ways we rarely pause to notice."Driven from his throne, yet never from his destiny's embrace,Humayun's return laid the path on which Akbar would leave his trace"Our journey will then continue to the Humayun's Tomb Museum — because no visit to the tomb is truly complete without experiencing the stories, artefacts, and narratives preserved there.Come, walk through history — and meet the emperor behind the monument.

name: From mehfils to movies: Courtesans of India
description: A Heritage Baithak at Qudsia BaghWhat happens when history remembers a woman only through the lens of morality and forgets her art, intellect, and influence?Join us for a fascinating journey into the world of India's courtesans—Nagarvadhus, Devadasis, Kanchanis, Tawaifs, and Nautch Girls—women who were once among the most accomplished artists, poets, musicians, patrons, and cultural icons of their age. Many enjoyed wealth, education, and social influence, supported temples and religious institutions, shaped artistic traditions, and moved comfortably within royal courts.Yet, with changing political and colonial attitudes, these women found themselves increasingly stigmatized, their identities reduced to stereotypes and their contributions pushed to the margins of history. We will trace this remarkable transformation and explore how many eventually carried their artistic legacy into the emerging world of Indian cinema, becoming singers, actors, dancers, and pioneers of the film industry.Through stories of power, performance, patronage, and prejudice, this walk seeks to rediscover the women who helped shape India's cultural landscape but whose voices have often been forgotten.Join Darwesh Heritage Walks at Qudsia Bagh as we uncover the extraordinary lives of India's courtesans—the custodians of art, culture, and memory.

name: Notes From The Modern Invention of Sexuality
description: Join us for a lecture that asks an unsettling question:What if modern society did not become obsessed with sex because it was finally liberated—but because it found new ways to make us confess?About the LectureWe tend to think of talking about sex as an act of freedom. The more openly we speak about our desires, identities, fantasies, and intimacies, the more liberated we become.Michel Foucault was deeply suspicious of this story.In The History of Sexuality, he makes a strange claim: modern societies did not develop through the repression of sex, but through an ever-expanding demand that people speak about it. What began as a religious obligation to confess gradually spread into schools, hospitals, psychiatric institutions, government bureaucracies, and eventually into everyday life itself.The result was not simply greater knowledge about sexuality. It was the creation of sexuality as a category through which people could be classified, monitored, governed, and understood.Drawing on Foucault's account of confession, power, and biopolitics, this lecture explores:How sexuality became a modern object of knowledgeWhy institutions are invested in making intimate life legibleWhat confession has to do with governance and social controlHow reality television, social media, dating apps, and digital platforms transform self-disclosure into a cultural normWhy every search, swipe, message, and online interaction increasingly resembles a confession to a machineAt a moment when we are constantly encouraged to "be ourselves," "share our truth," and "tell our story," this lecture asks a provocative question: when we finally believe we are expressing our most authentic selves, who is listening—and what are they doing with what we reveal?About the SpeakerDr. Ananya Dasgupta is a scholar of literature, film, philosophy, and psychoanalysis. She studied English Literature at Jadavpur University before completing her doctoral research through IIT Bombay and Monash University, Melbourne.Her work explores sexuality and publicness, feminist engagements with law, and the relationship between politics and affect in contemporary culture. Her doctoral research examined figures of extra-legality in Hindi cinema. Her public writing has appeared in Scroll, ASAP Art, and The Ladies Finger.She currently teaches at Ashoka University, where she continues to explore questions of desire, power, selfhood, and the strange ways institutions teach us to speak about ourselves.Your ticket includes a 250/- coupon redeemable on F&B at the venue. name: Among Behemoths: On Tigers, Territory, And People
description: Join us for a lecture that challenges one of the most enduring myths of the natural world:That tigers belong somewhere far away from us.About the LectureWhen we think of tigers, we often imagine a solitary predator lurking deep inside untouched forests — a powerful but distant creature whose world exists entirely apart from our own.Much of modern conservation has been built around this image.But the reality is far more complicated.Across India, tigers move through landscapes shaped not only by forests, but by roads, farms, villages, canals, and human lives. Their stories are intertwined with ours in ways that are often overlooked, revealing a world where conflict and coexistence exist side by side.Drawing on years of fieldwork and a lifetime spent exploring India's forests, this lecture looks beyond the mythology of the tiger to understand the animal itself — its behaviour, its adaptability, and its relationship with the people who share its territory.The session explores:Why many popular assumptions about tigers are misleadingHow tiger behaviour differs from the image of the relentless apex predatorThe changing meaning of "wild" in an increasingly human-shaped worldHow communities and tigers negotiate shared landscapesWhat conservation looks like beyond the idea of keeping nature separate from peopleMoving between ecology, anthropology, conservation, and storytelling, the lecture examines what happens when we stop thinking of wilderness as a place apart and begin seeing it as something deeply entangled with human life.At its core, this is a story about adaptation — not only of tigers, but of all of us.About the SpeakerRudraksha Rishi Mitra is a social innovator, researcher, and founder of Canopy Wild, an immersive wildlife travel and education company dedicated to deepening public engagement with conservation and the natural world.A graduate of the London School of Economics in International Social and Public Policy, he has worked extensively across public policy, sustainable development, and social entrepreneurship. In 2022, he was selected as an Unleash Global Talent, recognising him among the world's leading young social innovators.His research has focused on conservation policy and human–tiger interactions in the Terai Arc Landscape, combining ethnographic methods with ecological inquiry. Over the years, he has explored and documented tiger landscapes across India, including the Nilgiri Biosphere, Annamalais, Terai Arc, and the Dooars.Having spent much of his life among forests and wildlife habitats, he is particularly interested in the ways people and large carnivores continue to share space in a rapidly changing world.Ticket IncludesYour ticket includes a 500/- coupon for F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. name: Mode of transport - Ate glasshouse
description: Mode of Transport is the final stop of CTRLlab's Summer Coffee Crawl—an evening dedicated to journeys, movement, and the stories that connect people, places, and coffee across distances.As the series concludes, we turn our attention to the paths we travel: the routes coffee takes from farm to cup, the roads that lead us to new experiences, and the moments of movement that quietly shape our lives.Coffee Anchor follows coffee's remarkable journey from origin to cup, tracing the growers, traders, roasters, cafés, and communities that bring each bean across regions and continents before it reaches your hands. Together, we'll explore the networks, craftsmanship, and human stories that make coffee one of the world's most travelled beverages.Social Move invites guests to share stories from the road—memorable journeys, favourite commutes, unexpected detours, travel rituals, and the moments that altered our course. From everyday routes to life-changing adventures, we'll reflect on how movement often becomes the backdrop for our most meaningful memories.Hosted in an intimate setting designed for conversation and connection, the evening offers space to slow down, exchange stories, and appreciate the journeys—both ordinary and extraordinary—that bring us together.Summer Coffee Crawl 2025Stop 1 — Summer in the City14 June | Edit5, Sector 64, GurugramStop 2 — Coffee & Confessions21 June | Alayna Café, Shahpur JatStop 3 — Mode of Transport28 June | ATE Glasshouse, Lodhi RoadTickets- Single stop pass: Access to the event at that particular stop of the crawl- Three stop pass: Access to all 3 stops of the crawl
